coredata.dc:title: Visual lambda-cyhalothrin detection using yellow carbon dots molecularly imprinted polyvinylidene fluoride fluorescent membrane
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yahui
Liu, Yajie
Liu, Xinyu
Zhu, Yao
Yang, Yaling
Wang, Jing
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: Due to its common use as an insecticide during agricultural production, residual lambda-cyhalothrin (LC) in food and the environment presents a significant human health risk. Developing an accurate LC detection method that does not require pretreatment remains a challenge. This study developed a novel yellow carbon dots molecularly imprinted (YCDs@MIPs) polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) composite (YCDs@MIPs@PVDF) fluorescent membrane for rapid visual LC detection. This method required no pretreatments during analysis since the LC could be specifically adsorbed when the samples passed through the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane, causing fluorescence quenching, while matrix-interfering components could be removed for the porous structure of the membrane. A smartphone was used for rapid visual LC detection based on membrane fluorescence color changes in a dark-box ultraviolet analyzer. In food with a 15 % PVDF content and 6 mL added YCDs@MIPs, LC was quantitatively detected in a broad concentration range (5–500 μg/L) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.039 μg/L to 0.16 μg/L, which was lower than the strictest EU2019/92 limit of 10 μg/L. The analysis time was reduced from over 8 h when using the GB 23200.121–2021 and GB/T20769–2008 national standard methods to 30 min, while the analytical procedure decreased from six steps to two. The visual detection strategy combining the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF composite fluorescent membrane with a smartphone provided a new rapid detection technique for composite membrane purification and fluorescence signal output function.
coredata.dcterms:subject: YCDs@MIPs@PVDF
Membrane
Fluorescent
LC
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of the YCDs and YCDs@MIPs
Quantity optimization of PVDF and YCDs@MIPs
The fluorescence performance of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
The selectivity and stability of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
Visual detection via the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane combined with a smartphone
The fluorescence performance of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane towards LC
The selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
Visual LC detection in real samples via the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the detection principle.
The preparation process of the (A) YCDs-MIPs particles and (B) YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane. (C) A schematic diagram of the membrane formation mechanism.

The characterization of the YCDs, YCDs@MIPs, and the membranes. (A) The TEM images of the YCDs. (B) and (C) The SEM images of the YCDs@MIPs particles. (D) The FTIR spectra of the YCDs and YCDs@MIPs. (E) The surface microscopic morphology of the PVDF membrane. (F) The cross-sectional microscopic morphology of the PVDF membrane. (G) The surface microscopic morphology of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane. (H) The cross-sectional microscopic morphology of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane. (I) The images of the PVDF and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes at 365 nm under UV light.

(A) The dynamic adsorption of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane towards LC. The static adsorption of the (B) YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane and (C) YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membrane towards LC at different concentrations (5–200 μg/L). (D) The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra of the (a) LC, (b) YCDs, and (c) LC+YCDs, and (d) the YCD fluorescence emission spectra of YCDs. (E) The fluorescence responses of the YCDs@MIPs dispersed in different polar solvents. (F) A schematic illustration of the quenching mechanism.

(A) The chemical structures of LC and its analogs. (B) The fluorescence responses of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF and YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membranes after the addition of LC and its analogs. (C) The effect of foreign co-existing analogs with 1- and 10-fold LC concentrations.
Visual LC detection via the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane using a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp.

head.ce:title: Visual lambda-cyhalothrin detection using yellow carbon dots molecularly imprinted polyvinylidene fluoride fluorescent membrane

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China-Canada Joint Lab of Food Nutrition and Health (Beijing), School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China-Canada Joint Lab of Food Nutrition and Health (Beijing), School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China-Canada Joint Lab of Food Nutrition and Health (Beijing), School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to its common use as an insecticide during agricultural production, residual lambda-cyhalothrin (LC) in food and the environment presents a significant human health risk. Developing an accurate LC detection method that does not require pretreatment remains a challenge. This study developed a novel yellow carbon dots molecularly imprinted (YCDs@MIPs) polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) composite (YCDs@MIPs@PVDF) fluorescent membrane for rapid visual LC detection. This method required no pretreatments during analysis since the LC could be specifically adsorbed when the samples passed through the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane, causing fluorescence quenching, while matrix-interfering components could be removed for the porous structure of the membrane. A smartphone was used for rapid visual LC detection based on membrane fluorescence color changes in a dark-box ultraviolet analyzer. In food with a 15 % PVDF content and 6 mL added YCDs@MIPs, LC was quantitatively detected in a broad concentration range (5–500 μg/L) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.039 μg/L to 0.16 μg/L, which was lower than the strictest EU2019/92 limit of 10 μg/L. The analysis time was reduced from over 8 h when using the GB 23200.121–2021 and GB/T20769–2008 national standard methods to 30 min, while the analytical procedure decreased from six steps to two. The visual detection strategy combining the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF composite fluorescent membrane with a smartphone provided a new rapid detection technique for composite membrane purification and fluorescence signal output function.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lambda-cyhalothrin (LC) is widely used for pest control in fruit trees, vegetables, tea, and wheat. However, LC is toxic to the reproductive, immunity, and nervous systems of mammals [1,2]. It can accumulate in the body by consuming food containing LC residues, causing sub-chronic or chronic poisoning [3,4]. Since LC is often detected in various agricultural products [5–8], many countries have established strict regulations regarding their maximum residue limit (MRL). For example, the United States stipulates an LC MRL of 1 mg/kg and 0.02 mg/kg in rice, tubers, and bulbs, respectively [9]. China has established MRL values of 0.02–0.05 mg/kg in grains and 0.02–0.05 mg/kg in citrus fruits, nuts, and vegetables (GB2763–2019) [10]. The EU 2019/92 regulation adjusted the MRL of tea in 2019 from 1.0 mg/kg to 0.01 mg/kg. Therefore, developing a rapid, accurate method for detecting LC residues is essential for ensuring food safety.

Chromatography [11–13], immunoassays [14,15], and fluorescence [16,17] are currently the primary methods used for LC identification. As a traditional method for pesticide residue detection, chromatography is accurate, with a high recovery rate and good reproducibility. However, its application is significantly limited by expensive instruments, complex sample pre-processing, and time-consuming procedures. Luo et al. and Cui et al. used carbodiimide to synthesize LC immunogens and successfully prepare monoclonal and polyclonal LC antibodies. Luo et al. established ELISA limit of detection (LOD) values of 20 μg/kg, 30 μg/kg, and 60 μg/kg for apples, soybeans, and white lotus, respectively, while Cui et al. produced a value of 3.772 μg/L for Chinese cabbage. Although ELISA realized convenient, rapid detection, some challenges remained, such as high false positives and difficulty in obtaining antigen antibodies.

Fluorescent nanoparticle molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been widely applied for rapid detection due to the superior optical properties of fluorescent nanoparticles and the high selectivity of MIPs [18]. The interaction between the target and the active imprinted site changes the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent nanoparticles. Therefore, the target can be quantitatively analyzed by monitoring the fluorescence signal changes. Gao et al. fabricated a SiO2-MPS@FMIP (MPS: 3-(methacryloxyl) propyl trimethoxysilane and FMIP: fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer) and a novel allyl fluorescent@MIPs to detect trace amounts of LC in solvents and honey, at LODs of 0.0037 nM and 0.004 nm, respectively [19,20]. Wang et al. prepared a hollow LC-imprinted fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) polymeric nanoparticle as a fluorescent sensor for efficient LC detection in Beijing-Hangzhou Grand Canal water [21]. Increasingly diverse fluorescent@MIPs are being developed as highly selective sensors for LC identification in water and some food samples [22–24]. However, their application for rapid LC detection in complex food matrices remains challenging since the purification process is complicated, inaccurate, and inconvenient. Therefore, the rapid, simple, and effective removal of interfering substances from food is essential.

Membrane technology, which can separate different components at the molecular level, is currently widely used in food processing, water treatment, and drug purification due to its physical processing properties, environmental friendliness, and excellent purification functionality [25–27]. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is the most commonly used polymer for ultrafiltration (UF) and microfiltration (MF) membranes due to its excellent chemical resistance, heat and hydrolytic stability, and good mechanical properties [28,29].

Loading fluorescent@MIPs onto a membrane provides a new method for rapid determination. Herein, a novel yellow-emission carbon dots (YCDs) molecularly imprinted polyvinylidene fluoride (YCDs@MIPs@PVDF) membrane is proposed for rapid LC detection in food, combining the separation function of membranes and the specific fluorescence change function of YCDs@MIPs. The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane adsorbs the LC while other interfering components in the food are removed. The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane is placed in a dark-box ultraviolet analyzer for visual, quantitative LC detection, while the photos are taken using a smartphone. Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the detection principle. This research promotes fast LC detection in food and provides a new method for quick, accurate determination in other fields, such as environmental and biochemical testing.

Standard LC, cypermethrin (CYP), cyfluthrin (CYF), deltamethrin (DEL), and fenvalerate (FEN) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Beijing Mairuida Technology Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). The McLean Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) provided the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), Triton X-100, tetraethyl silicate (TEOS), methanol, acetonitrile, and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). Deionized water (Watsons) was used for all experiments.

The structure and morphology of the PVDF and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes were observed via scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA3 LM, Czechoslovakia, Shanghai), while transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL F2000, Japan) was used for the YCD morphology. The YCDs@MIPs, YCDs@MIPs@PVDF, and PVDF membrane structures were examined via Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Vertex 70, Bruker GmbH, Germany). A DF-101S constant-temperature heating magnetic stirrer was used to dissolve the polymer solution. A dark-box ultraviolet analyzer (EZF-7, Wenzhou) for fluorescence stability determination and visualization. The fluorescence intensity of the YCDs@MIPs and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FS5, U.K.) at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra of the LC, YCDs, and LC/YCD combination were measured using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Cary 100 UV-Vis, UK). A JA5003 electronic balance was used to accurately weigh the reagents. This study fabricated an automatic circulating filtration membrane device. The LC content was detected via high-performance liquid chromatography (Waters) with an Inertsil ODS-SP (4.6 mm×250 mm, 5 μm) column (Shimadzu Co) during a controlled experiment (Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of the YCDs and YCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The YCDs were prepared using a previously described method with some modifications, as delineated in the Supporting Information [23]. The YCDs@MIPs nanospheres were prepared via reverse microemulsion. First, 7.5 mL of cyclohexane and 1.8 mL of Triton X-100 were dispersed in 25 mL of cyclohexane and subjected to vigorous magnetic stirring for 15 min at room temperature. Then, 1 mL of YCDs, 50 μL of TEOS, and 100 μL of an aqueous ammonia solution (25 wt %) were added and stirred for 2 h in the dark. Next, 25 μL of APTES and 100 μL of LC dissolved in 10 mg/mL methanol were added and stirred for 24 h in the dark. After polymerization, the mixture was washed with acetone and centrifuged for 10 min to remove the supernatant. The precipitate was washed three times with deionized water. The polymer was further eluted with methanol:acetonitrile=1:2 (v/v) at room temperature for 48 h to remove the LC templates completely. The final YCDs@MIPs were obtained after drying in a vacuum oven at 50℃ for 24 h. The non-imprinted polymers (YCDs@NIPs) were prepared using the same procedure but without LC.

The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane was prepared via phase inversion. Here, 5 mg of the dried molecularly imprinted quantum dots were dissolved in 1 mL DMF. Then, 2 mL of a membrane casting solution consisting of PVDF (15 %, by weight of the solution) and PVP (2 %) was added and dissolved at about 80℃ for 3 h while stirring vigorously. The casting solutions were scraped onto a clean glass plate using a 150 μm casting knife and evaporated for 30 s before being immersed in a deionized water coagulation bath. The PVDF membrane was prepared in the same way as the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane but without the YCDs@MIPs particles.

The APTES, TEOS, YCDs, and YCDs@MIPs were examined via FTIR. All samples were ground thoroughly and pressed into tablets before FTIR analysis. Each sample was scanned at least three times in a range of 500–4000 cm-1. The YCD microstructures were observed via TEM, while those of the YCDs@MIPs, PVDF, and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes were observed using SEM. The membrane cross-sections were prepared via fracturing in liquid nitrogen and sprayed with gold before analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantity optimization of PVDF and YCDs@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The membrane pore size was in reverse proportion to the PVDF concentration, which was relative to the fluorescence signal sensitivity by affecting the interfering component filtration in the sample. The YCDs@MIPs content affected the accuracy and sensitivity of the fluorescence signal. The optimal PVDF and YCDs@MIPs composition was determined by evaluating the influence of different PVDF percentages (8 %, 10 %, 13 %, 15 %, and 18 %) and YCDs@MIPs content levels (1 mg, 3 mg, 5 mg, 7 mg, and 10 mg) on the fluorescence quenching constant (KSV) of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane, using the automatic circulating filtration device. The Stern-Volmer equation was used to describe the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane fluorescence quenching.
F
0
/
F =
K
SV
[ LC ]
where F and F0 denote the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of LC, respectively, and Ksv represents the quenching constant.

The automatic circulating filtration device was used to determine the separation performance of the YCDs@MIPs@PVD (15 % PVDF level and a 6 mL YCDs@MIPs addition concentration) and PVDF membranes and evaluate their porosity(ε), pure water flux (J), and rejection (R). The detailed calculation data of these parameters are provided in the Supporting Information (Eq. 1 to Eq. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence performance of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence performance of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF and YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membranes was determined using the automatic circulating filtration device. During the dynamic adsorption experiment, 100 μg/L of the LC standard solution was placed in the feed bottle of the device for replete adsorption at 0.2 MPa for 40 min at a flow rate of 2.0 mL/min, after which the fluorescence response was recorded at 420 nm. The static adsorption experiment used a series of LC standard solutions ranging from 5 to 200 μg/L, while the fluorescence changes were recorded in the absence and presence of LC at 0.2 MPa to determine the dynamic adsorption saturation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and stability of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF and YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membranes were determined using LC and its structural analog. The selectivity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane was characterized using the CYP, CYF, DEL, and FEN structural analogs at the same concentrations and a 10-fold LC concentration of 200 μg/L. 15 interfering substances including K+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, nitrate, sulfate, threonine, phenylalanine, leucine, valine, and VC with concentration of 1.0×10-2 mol/L were selected to observe the selectivity of the YCDs@MIPs membrane. The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane were stored away from light for 50 d, while the fluorescence intensity changes were measured every 5 d. Then, it was placed in a dark-box ultraviolet analyzer equipped with a 365 nm ultraviolet light source, and the fluorescence intensity changes were recorded every 30 min within 120 min of continuous irradiation with the ultraviolet lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection via the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane combined with a smartphone

ce:section.ce:para: The cucumbers, apples, and Chinese cabbage were purchased from a market. Here, 1 mg/mL of the LC standard solution was diluted with the blank matrix solution to prepare a series of LC matrix standard samples with LC concentration between 5 μg/L and 500 μg/L. 5 g of the washed sample was crushed, adding 0.05, 1.50 and 2.25 mL 1 mg/mL of the LC standard solution. The supernatant obtained via vortex centrifugation was placed in the automatic circulating filtration device for purification by the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane at 0.2 MPa for 22 min at a flow rate of 2 mL/min.

The adsorbed YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane was placed in the UV light box to capture images of the fluorescence color changes at different LC concentrations using a smartphone with a color analysis app (colorimetric titration developed at the University of Burgos) for accurate visual LC detection. The app showed the calculated concentrations of the test samples versus the different color parameters (R, G, B, H, S, V, RGB, and ∆RGB). The best-fitting curve with the highest R2 parameter appeared in the first position. The recovery and LOD values of the visual YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane combined with the smartphone method were investigated at three different addition levels. HPLC was used for further verification and comparison.

Fig. 2(A), (B), and (C) show the schematic diagram of the YCDs, YCDs-MIPs. and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane preparation processes, while Fig. S1 illustrates the YCD synthesis process. A one-pot reaction was employed to obtain YCDs@MIPs. The polymer was obtained via reverse-phase microemulsion polymerization. In the presence of a cross-linker, APTES, a functional monomer, and LC recognized each other via hydrogen bond cross-linking, after which TEOS was added as an initiator. The hydrolytic condensation reaction with APTES on the surfaces of the CDs created a SiO2 coating. The LC template molecules were removed via elution, followed by 3D pore formation on the YCDs@MIPs surface with specific LC recognition capability (Fig. 2(A)).

TEOS acted as a crosslinker during the imprinting process, binding functional monomers to template molecules, which impacted the YCDs@MIPs adsorption and selectivity. As presented in Fig. S2A, the volume ratio of template-to-crosslinker volume ratio (LC: TEOS = 1: 0.2, 1: 0.5, 1: 1, and 1:2) was investigated (Fig. S2A), showing that the fluorescence change (F0/F) was strongest at a 1:0.5 ratio. A lower LC/TEOS ratio may cause an excessively thick imprint coating layer on the YCDs@MIPs, affecting its fluorescence quenching sensitivity. Therefore, a ratio of 1:0.5 was selected as optimal for subsequent experiments.

As a luminescent material, the CD dosage significantly affected the analytical performance of YCDs@MIPs. The F0/F of YCDs@MIPs at different LC concentrations increased gradually at higher CD levels ranging from 2 mg/mL to 10 mg/mL, followed by a decrease (Fig. S2B). This may be related to a decline in the MIP layer thickness, leading to fewer binding sites.

The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane was prepared by mixing the YCDs@MIPs particles in the casting solution using the phase transformation method (Fig. 2(B)). Membrane pore formation via phase transformation was caused by interdiffusion on the interface. After immersing the casting solution in the coagulation water bath, the solvent and non-solvent diffused and exchanged mass through the casting solution/coagulation bath interface. The casting solution became a thermodynamically unstable system when the exchange reached a certain level, causing phase separation and membrane pore formation (Fig. 2(C)). The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane displayed a strong fluorescence signal and provided a specific LC recognition site.

The membrane pores decreased at higher PVDF levels. The membrane pore sizes were related to the removal of the matrix interference components, which affected the target detection sensitivity and accuracy. Excessively low YCDs@MIPs particle content significantly weakened the membrane fluorescence signal. An overabundance of YCDs@MIPs particles decreased the detection sensitivity and accuracy since their distribution inside the membrane pores generated blockages, resulting in relatively insignificant fluorescence changes caused by LC adsorption. Therefore, both the PVDF and YCDs@MIPs levels in the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane should be optimized (Figs. S3A and S3B). A higher KSV increased the degree of quenching, indicating that the membrane was more sensitive for LC detection.

The KSV of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane displayed an initial increase, followed by a decline at a higher PVDF content, reaching a maximum value of 0.00246 at a PVDF level of 15 %. When the PVDF content increased to 18 %, the LC molecules passed through the large membrane pores before adsorption by the YCDs@MIPs, some of which were encapsulated by the polymer, reducing the membrane sensitivity (Fig. S3A). Adding a higher YCDs@MIPs quantity initially increased the KSV of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane, followed by a decline, reaching a maximum value of 0.00257 at an added YCDs@MIPs concentration of 5 mg (Fig. 4(B)). Therefore, the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane displayed optimal detection sensitivity at a PVDF level of 15 % and an added YCDs@MIPs concentration of 5 mg.

Both the YCDs and YCDs@MIPs particles were relatively uniformly spherical, with average particle sizes of about 2.5 nM and 234 nM. Fig. S4 shows the particle size distribution of the YCDs and YCDs@MIPs. The YCDs were effectively wrapped in the YCDs@MIPs. Therefore, the YCDs@MIPs diameters were significantly larger than those of the YCDs ( Fig. 3(A), (B), and (C)).

The synthesis and functional surface groups of the YCDs and YCDs@MIPs were examined via FTIR (Fig. 3(D)), presenting characteristic peaks at 1560.3 cm-1, which corresponded to CO (carboxylic groups) band stretching vibration. Hydrogen bonding occurred between the carboxyl group hydrogen and the fluorine atom on the LC template, leading to π-π electron cloud stacking between the carbonyl section of the carboxyl group and the benzene ring on the LC template molecule. Therefore, the interaction force between the YCD and template molecules provided specific binding sites in YCDs@MIP. The YCDs@MIP displayed new characteristic peaks that differed from the YCDs, which denoted the imprinted polymer. The adsorption peaks at 2928.4 cm-1 (–CH3), 1100.7 cm-1, and 955.4 cm-1 (–Si-O) represented APTES and TEOS, while that at 1630.7 cm-1 (–NH) denoted APTES. These peaks confirmed the presence of considerable -Si existed on the crosslinker surface, which could easily form Van der Waals forces with the –F groups in the template molecules to form specific binding sites. A characteristic YCDs@MIP peak at 1073.8 cm-1 corresponded to the Si-O-Si group stretching vibration. Fig. S5 shows the FTIR spectra of the enlarged local areas.

Fig. 3(E), (F), (G), and (H) show the SEM microstructures of the PVDF and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes, of which Fig. 3(E) and (G) illustrate the surface morphologies. The YCDs@MIPs particles were mainly distributed on the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane surface, which enhanced the fluorescence signals and detection accuracy results. Compared with the PVDF membrane, the membrane porosity and pore sizes of the added YCDs@MIPs surface particles increased. Fig. 3(F) and (H) show the cross sections of the PVDF and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes. The pores of both membranes appeared sponge-like, while the holes in the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane seemed larger and somewhat defective. The addition of hydrophilic YCDs@MIPs particles accelerated phase separation and effectively prevented PVDF macromolecule aggregation, which increased the membrane porosity and pore size [30]. Moreover, particle addition increased the casting solution viscosity, which inhibited the movement of large molecular chains and caused surface and internal pore structure defects in the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane.
The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane appeared blue after YCDs@MIPs particle addition (Fig. 3(I)), which faded after LC adsorption under 365 nm UV light, enabling visual detection.

The pure water flux of the optimized PVDF and YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes reached equilibrium in 40 min at about 164.75 Lm-2h-1 and 294.28 Lm-2h-1, respectively (Fig. S6A). The higher YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane water flux could be attributed to a larger pore size, which was consistent with the SEM results. The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane displayed a higher porosity than the PVDF membrane (Fig. S6B). This was because YCDs@MIPs particle addition accelerated the solvent and non-solvent exchange during membrane phase transformation, increasing its porosity. The rejection of YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane to BSA was 40.53 %, which was lower than that of PVDF membrane of 85.53 % for the larger pore size and higher porosity of YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane (Fig. S6B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence performance of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane towards LC
ce:section.ce:para: The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane reached a dynamic LC adsorption equilibrium of 92.43 % at 22 min ( Fig. 4(A)). The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane displayed a rapid transfer rate during the first 9 min with 75 % LC adsorption, which reached a maximum at 22 min, where it stabilized. Therefore, the subsequent YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane filtration conditions were set at 22 min.

The fluorescence intensity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF and YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membranes towards a series of standard LC solutions in the automatic filtration device was measured after 22 min. At 535 nm, the emission intensity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane decreased gradually at a higher LC concentration, while the YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membrane remained mostly unchanged (Fig. 4(B) and (C)). The KSV value of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane was 0.0028 μg/L, which was 3.6-fold higher than that of the YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membrane, showing the high LC sensitivity and specificity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane. A comparison between the IF values in this paper and previous studies indicated the superior specificity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane (Table S1).

The UV absorption spectra of LC and YCDs, as well as the fluorescence emission spectra of the YCDs, were measured to determine the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membrane (Fig. 4(D)). The maximum UV absorption wavelength of LC was 210 nm, which was close to that of the YCDs (325 nm). LC addition did not change the maximum UV absorption wavelength of the YCDs. In addition, the UV absorption values of LC were far removed from the fluorescence emission spectrum of the YCDs (535 nm), which met the condition of inter-spectral photo-induced charge transfer.

The fluorescence intensity of the YCDs@MIP dispersed in various solvents was measured to investigate the impact of solvent polarity (Fig. 4(E)). Since the solvents did not affect the fluorescence intensity of the luminescence, they were not responsible for quenching. Therefore, it is speculated that inter-spectral photo-induced charge transfer may occur between the YCDs and LC. As electron donors, the YCDs transferred their excited electrons from the conduction band to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the LC in inter-spectral photo-induced conditions, causing LC fluorescence quenching by YCDs@MIPs [31–33]. Fig. 4(F) shows a schematic illustration of the quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: on the fluorescence quenching ability of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane. （D）The effect of vitamin C and metal ions

The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane selectivity for structural analogs such as CYD, CYP, DEL, and FEN was investigated ( Fig. 5). At the same concentration, the KSV of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane towards LC was significantly higher than for its structural analogs, indicating its specificity towards LC. However, the KSV of the YCDs@NIPs@PVDF membrane towards LC was almost the same as for its structural analogs (Fig. 5(B)). The F0/F of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane remained mostly unaffected in the presence of co-existing analogs at 1- and 10-fold LC concentrations (Fig. 5(C)). The trifluoromethyl group in the LC displayed strong Van der Waals force interaction with the silicon atom at the YCDs@MIPs imprinting site and matched the spatial structure of the imprint hole. Therefore, their effective association can inhibit adsorption to other analogs. Some vitamin C and metal ions were selected to observe the selectivity of the YCDs@MIPs membrane, as shown in Fig. 5(D). It showed basically no impact on the fluorescence intensity of the YCDs@MIPs membrane with vitamin C or metal ions. Membrane pore sieving effect and the selectivity of YCDs@MIPs particles in the membrane made the matrix interfering components directly penetrated the membrane. The optical stability and reproducibility of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane were also investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane remained mostly unchanged over a period of 50 d (Fig. S7A), suggesting stable YCDs@MIPs particle binding in the PVDF membrane. Furthermore, the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane fluorescence did not change with time and continuous 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. S7B). The superior fluorescence stability indicated its commercial application value. The F0/F values of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane were also assessed via a successive removal-rebinding cycle test to determine its reproducibility (Fig. S8). After five repeated experiments of the same batch, the detection performance was almost the same as in the initial test, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.4 %, illustrating its excellent reproducibility (Fig. S8A). The YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane coming from three different batches showed excellent reproducibility too (Fig. S8B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual LC detection in real samples via the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Real food samples such as apples, cucumbers, and Chinese cabbage were purchased from a market to investigate the application of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane combined with a smartphone. No LC was detected in these samples, and spiked recovery tests were performed. The fluorescence of the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membranes were gradually quenched with LC concentration increasing, resulting different YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane colors in dark box ultraviolet, so, visual LC detection via the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane using a smartphone under a 365 nm UV lamp was achieved ( Fig. 6). The B, V, ΔRGB, and RGB parameters of the images relative to the LC content in apple were examined. B showed the best linear relationship with the LC concentration in a 5–500 μg/L range and was selected as the measurement parameter (Table S2). The ability of the visual YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane and HPLC methods to detect LC in different foods were compared. The visual detection method exhibited satisfactory recovery rates in the food samples, ranging from 92.93 % to 117.57 %, while the RSD values were between 2.1 % and 5.3 % (Table S3), which were consistent with the HPLC results. Furthermore, the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane was compared with other reported methods for LC detection (Table S4). The results indicated that it could be applied as a superior rapid detection method in foods, exhibiting high accuracy and sensitivity without the necessity for pretreatment.

This study investigates a novel YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane combined with a smartphone for rapid visual LC detection in food. The entire detection process is completed in 30 min without sample purification pretreatment, while the analytical procedure was reduced from more than six steps to two compared with GB 23200.121–2021 and GB/T20769–2008 national standard methods. The high accuracy of this method is attributed to the specific MIP target recognition sites, the sensitive YCDs@MIPs fluorescence response, and the purification function of the membrane. The satisfactory linear relationship indicates that LC can be quantitatively detected in a broad linear range (5–500 μg/L) at an ultralow LOD of 0.18 μg//kg, which is lower than the strictest EU2019/92 limit of 10 μg/L. The method shows remarkable sensing performance at three different addition levels, with recovery rates ranging from 92.93 % to 117.57 %. This research provides a new technique for pesticide residue detection, presenting a fast on-site response and high specificity and sensitivity, showing excellent prospects for practical applications.

Xinyu Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yao Zhu: Visualization, Software, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yaling Yang: Data curation. Jing Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization. Baoguo Sun: Supervision, Project administration. Yahui He: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yajie Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.
All authors contributed to writing this manuscript. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Yahui He reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32102110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The actual video of the visual detection using the YCDs@MIPs@PVDF membrane (MP4). The experimental details regarding the YCD synthesis process, PVDF and YCD contents optimization, parameter calculation, comparison of the developed method with other methods, and validation (PDF). Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136041.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Aydin, S. Dinckan, S.N.K. Elmas, T. Savran, F.N. Arslan, I. Yilmaz, A novel phenolphthalein-based fluorescent sensor for Al3+ sensing in drinking water and herbal tea samples, Food Chemistry, 337(2021).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yahui He, (1981-), female, associate professor, doctor's degree, received her Ph.D. in the Hefei University of Technology at 2008. Following graduation, she worked in the School of Food and Health of Beijing Technology and Business University. She is now an associate professor at the School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Technology University. Her research interests include rapid analysis for toxic chemicals (such as pesticides and metallic ion), residue analysis: the enrichment and separation technology of trace-level contaminants in food, molecularly imprinted polymer, food safety and quality control, risk assessment of food contaminants, and fate of contaminants during food processing.

Yajie Liu, (1997-), female, master’s degree, is currently a master course student in the School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University. Her research interests focused simultaneous and rapid detection of advanced glycation end products in fo

Xinyu Liu, (1998-), female, master’s degree, graduated from the School of Food and Health of Beijing Technology and Business University as a master in 2023. Her research interests include the rapid detection of food pesticides and metal ions in paper substrates.

Yao Zhu, (1996-), female, master’s degree, is currently a master course student in the School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University. Her research interests focused on the rapid detection of harmful materials in foods.

Yaling Yang, (2001-), female, master’s degree, is currently a master course student in the School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University. Her research interests focused on the rapid detection of metal ions in foods.

Jing Wang, (1976-), female, professor, doctor's degree, received her Ph.D. in the Jiangnan University at 2003 and is a visiting scholar at Clemson University in the United States. She is now a professor at the School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Technology University. His research interests include application of biotechnology in food industry, deep processing and comprehensive utilization of functional food, agricultural and sideline products.

Baoguo Sun, (1961-), male, professor, doctor's degree, received his Ph.D. in the Department of Chemical Engineering of Tsinghua University. He is now holding a Professorship in Beijing Technology and Business University, and recognized as a Fellow of Chinese Academy of Engineering since Dec. of 2009. His research interests include constructed a molecular characteristic structural unit model of meat flavor sulfur compounds, manufacture of 3-furan sulfide series and asymmetric disulfide food spices, meat flavor manufacturing technology using livestock and poultry meat, bone, and fat as the main raw materials, research on Functional Factors in Baijiu, and research on the Flavor of Baijiu.
